Three objects
I keep thinking
about

I came into ET5131 expecting to learn business frameworks. What I did not expect was to find myself genuinely changed by them. These are the three things that did it.

01 The Filter Mindset 02 The Whiteboard Knowledge 03 The Chair Skills
The team · ET5131 · NTU · 2026
Object 01 of 03

The Filter

Mindset

Our first idea was a microplastic water filter. Sustainability-aligned, technically interesting, socially conscious. It fell apart within a week when research showed NEWater already solves the problem better than we ever could, and that none of us had the capability to compete.

Prof. Ang's framing landed hard: entrepreneurship is not about solving interesting problems. It is about solving problems that enough people will open their wallets for. If nobody pays, it is just an idea. An opportunity requires timing, team capability, and a real paying problem. Miss any one and you have a project, not a startup.

What the filter taught me was a new discipline: asking not just whether something is a good idea, but whether it is a pursuable opportunity for this team, right now. Opportunity recognition is iterative. It requires killing ideas you are attached to when the data does not support them. The discomfort of that is the mindset shift.

The filter did not work. But learning to filter ideas honestly, before committing to them, was the first real thing I built this semester.

The opportunity test
⌛
Timing
Right moment?
👪
Team
Can we pursue?
💰
Paying problem
Will they pay?
Miss any one and you have a project, not a startup
Object 02 of 03

The Whiteboard

Knowledge

Prof. Lerwen Liu's Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Framework changed the unit of analysis. The pillars are WHY, WHAT, HOW, WHO, and IMPACT. Before this module I was building PawMatch as a product. After it, I understood I was building a node in a network: AVS, HDB, SPCA, SOSD, corporate partners, shelter staff, adopters. Each one a stakeholder with their own incentives and their own definition of value.

BugBoom sharpened this further. Regulatory pressure in Singapore forced them to develop a chemical pre-treatment process that competitors in less regulated environments had no reason to build. The constraint became the moat. The most defensible parts of a business are often built not from ambition, but from necessity.

Kaden from Moonbeam added the customer discovery piece. Moonbeam stops leading with the sustainability narrative and lets the product speak first, because customers form opinions based on experience rather than story. The ASPCA research validating personality-based matching should have been our starting point for PawMatch. It came near the end. A costly sequence to get wrong.

Prof. Lerwen Liu's Cultural Intelligence framework across its four dimensions of motivation, knowledge, strategy, and behaviour also reframed something I thought I already understood. How people process direction differently is not a soft skill. It is a systems skill.

The whiteboard is where you draw the map. What this module taught me is that the map has to include everyone who matters, not just the people you are building for.

Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Framework · Prof. Lerwen Liu · ET5131
Business Model Canvas mapped to the WHY, WHAT, HOW, WHO framework · ET5131
Moonbeam
Upcycles spent coffee grounds and brewer grains into high-fibre baked goods. Leads with taste, not sustainability.
BugBoom
Food waste composting startup. Regulatory constraints forced proprietary tech. Constraint became moat.
Object 03 of 03

The Chair

Skills

Tasks were assigned clearly. Deadlines were set. What consistently happened was that the following week, little or nothing had moved, and no one had said anything. No message asking for help, no flag that they were stuck. Just silence, until the day before submission when questions would suddenly fill the chat.

I absorbed the work. I built the entire executive summary website alone: problem statement, algorithm, prototype showcase, roadmap. The concern raised afterwards was not about two weeks of inaction. It was about my initiative.

Taha from BugBoom put the design principle plainly: if your system requires consistent correct behaviour from users, it will fail. You have to design for what people actually do. I set direction clearly. I did not design a system that made accountability the default. Psychological safety does not emerge from goodwill. It has to be deliberately built from the first meeting.

The communication failures that were genuinely mine: a same-day meeting during break week that half the team could not attend, a direction change communicated in a document without enough context. I own those. But the deeper lesson was about reading the room and noticing the silence before it became a pattern.

Telling people where to sit is not the same as making them want to be there.

Mindset, Knowledge, Skills · Entrepreneurship Ecosystem Framework · ET5131
What I should have built from day one
Direct check-ins, not just session recaps
Sub-tasks broken down further, not sections to own independently
Psychological safety built deliberately from the first meeting
Structures that make it easy to say you are behind
To close

ET5131 gave me
more than
frameworks.

It gave me tools I stress-tested against something real, with real stakes, in real time. Prof. Ang's advice stays with me: try your absolute best, and if something fails, know there is always another way around the problem. That is not consolation. It is a discipline.

PawMatch is a proof of concept built mostly by one person in a team of six. That is also a lesson. Validate before building. Set team culture before work begins. Design systems that make accountability the default. The gap between someone who can execute and someone who can lead is what I am working to close.

Chua Victor
ET5131 · Prof. Lerwen Liu · Nanyang Technological University
Three lessons that stayed
Mindset
Validate before building. Kill ideas when the data does not support them.
Knowledge
Map the ecosystem. Constraints are often where the moat is built.
Skills
Design accountability into the structure. Goodwill is not enough.
